revision.text:
== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] <references group="注釈"/>[[ja:沖宮]]

== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}}
[[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] <references group="注釈"/>[[ja:沖宮]]

{{Shrine | Name = Oki Shrine | Image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | Location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 40 | Longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | Map = Japan Naha city | Deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | Shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | Founded = | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | Festival = | Rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Oki Shrine | Image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | Location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | Latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | Longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 40 | Longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | Country code = JP | Map = Japan Naha city | Deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | Shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | Founded = | Main hall = | Other names = | Notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | Festival = | Rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] == Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]]{{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Shrine | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 40 | Longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Shinto Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] to [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 40 | Longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Okinawa Shrine|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku Shrine|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Shrine | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | Latitude minutes = 12 | Latitude seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | Longitude minutes = 40 | Longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina Shrine|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi Shrine|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato Hachiman Shrine]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|1625|8=en|10=ja|11=尚豊王}}, it became customary to visit the sacred family shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|New|8=en|10=ja|11=尚賢王}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|8=en|10=ja|11=羽地朝秀}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} It is believed that through these events, the sacred family shrines became {{ill|official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|ja|波上宮}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū‎|ja|八幡宮}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|沖宮}}, and {{ill|Kinkannon Shrine|ja|金武宮}}. All of them belong to the holy family lineage. However, since Kinkannon Shrine did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮}} (later {{ill|Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社}}) and {{ill|Tenmangu Shrine|ja|天満宮}}, which are not of the holy family lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} {{Refn|In mainland Japan, many {{ill|kanpei-sha|ja:官幣社}} shrines are associated with the imperial family or powerful clans through blood relations or ancestral ties. However, in Ryukyu, which had an imported religion, there are no such traditions. It is also characteristic that the establishment tales of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines often involve some kind of spiritual phenomenon.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73}}。|group=注釈}} [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}}[[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]]== Content taken from [https://shinto.miraheze.org/wiki/History_of_Shinto_Shrines_in_Okinawa?oldid=135194 History of Shinto Shrines in Okinawa] == However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球侵攻}} in 1609, shrines of the sacred lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|8=en|10=ja|11=聞得大君}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by sacred family shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples.
