Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]][[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:Https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}{{Ryukyu Eight Shrines}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}[[Category:new pages]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Standalone Shrine]] {{Okinawan Shrines}}

revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}}[[Category:new pages]] {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Former unranked shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto Shrines]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[Category:History of Shinto shrines in Okinawa and forks]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Oki-no-gū-qq]] [[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:Https://zh.wikipedia.org/wiki/]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qq]][[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Standalone Shrines]] {{Okinawan Shrines}}

This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui
