{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written 2]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category: 2]] [[Category:Pages to be written]][[Category: 2]]

According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3年, 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]]

Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}}

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Oki-no-gū}}

It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not ranked in the [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{citation needed|Oki-no-gū}}

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{citation needed|Oki-no-gū}}

revision.comment: 56 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:沖宮 up to rev 95809386
revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 00:28 (UTC)|ソートキー=沖縄おきのくう}} {{独自研究|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 00:28 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 沖宮 |画像 = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] |所在地 = [[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]奥武山町44 | 緯度度 = 26 | 緯度分 = 12 | 緯度秒 = 10.0 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 127 |経度分 = 40 | 経度秒 = 37.5 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP | 地図 = Japan Naha city |祭神 = 天受久女龍宮王御神
天龍大御神
天久臣乙女王御神
熊野三神 |社格 = 旧[[無格社]] |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''沖宮'''（おきのぐう）は[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つであるが、[[近代社格制度]]では社格を与えられておらず、神社本庁に属さない[[単立]]の神社である。（[[無格社]]）。[[奥武山公園]]の東部に位置する。 == 祭神 == 天受久女龍宮王御神（てんじゅくめりゅうぐうおうおんかみ。[[天照大御神]]）・天龍大御神（てんりゅうおおおんかみ）・天久臣乙女王御神（あめくしんおとめおうおんかみ）・熊野三神（[[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]]・[[熊野権現|速玉男尊]]・[[事解男尊]]）を祀る。 [[明治]]の[[神仏分離]]までは「沖山三所権現」（沖の寺）と称し、[[神仏習合]]で[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[薬師如来]]・[[十一面観音]]を祭っていた。 *神代一代 **相殿中央の御座 *** 御先 女天神代　天受久女龍宮大御神（てんじゅくめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） *** 中　女天神代　天智門女龍宮大御神（あまちじょうめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） *** 今　女天神代　天受賀女龍宮大御神（てんじゅかめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） **相殿左の御座 ** 父御神　天龍大御神（てんりゅうおおおんかみ） ** 母御神　天久臣乙女大御神（あめくしんおとめおおおんかみ） **相殿右の御座 *** 天芳子乙女大御神（てんぽうしおとめおおおんかみ） *** 天仁子乙女大御神（てんじんしおとめおおおんかみ） *** 天来子乙女大御神（てんらいしおとめおおおんかみ） **熊野三神 *** 伊弉冊尊（いざなみのみこと） *** 速玉男尊（はやたまをのみこと） *** 事解男尊（ことさかをのみこと） == 摂末社 == ; 末社 住吉神社 : 神代二代 : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 天風龍大神（あまふうりゅうおおかみ） - 子（ね）の神 表筒王 :* 天火龍大神（あまひりゅうおおかみ） - 丑（うし）の神 中筒王 : : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 表臣幸乙女大御神（うはしんこうおとめおおおんかみ） :* 中臣幸乙女大御神 : : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 天水龍大神（あますいりゅうおおかみ） - 寅（とら）の神 底筒王 :* 底臣幸乙女王御神（そこしんこうおとめおおおんかみ） ; 末社 辨財天宮（べんざいてんぐう） : 神世三代 :* 木龍宇具志久乙姫王（もくりゅうぐしおとひめおおお） - 辰（たつ）の神 :*: またの名を 辨天負百津姫神（べんてんよもつひめかみ）：ノロ神の御名 == 歴史 == 創建の由緒は不詳だが、[[源為朝]]の時代と史料にある{{efn|[[琉球王国]]の[[正史]]である『[[中山世鑑]]』を始め、『[[おもろさうし]]』、『[[鎮西琉球記]]』などでは、源為朝は現在の沖縄県の地に逃れ、その子が琉球王家の始祖[[舜天]]になったとされる。この話がのちに[[曲亭馬琴]]の『[[椿説弓張月]]』を産んだ。[[日琉同祖論]]と関連づけて語られる事が多く、この話に基づき、[[大正]]11年（1922）には為朝上陸の碑が建てられた。表側に「上陸の碑」と刻まれて、その左斜め下にはこの碑を建てることに尽力した[[東郷平八郎]]の名が刻まれている。なお、『[[中山世鑑]]』を編纂した[[羽地朝秀]]は、摂政就任後の[[康熙]]12年（[[寛文]]13年（1673））3月の『仕置（しおき）』（令達及び意見を記し置きした書）で、琉球の人々の祖先は、かつて日本から渡来してきたのであり、また有形無形の名詞はよく通じるが、話し言葉が日本と相違しているのは、遠国のため交通が長い間途絶えていたからであると語り、源為朝が王家の祖先だというだけでなく琉球の人々の祖先が日本からの渡来人であると述べている（真境名安興『真境名安興全集』第一巻19頁参照。元の文は「窃かに惟ふは此国人生初は、日本より渡りたる儀疑い無く御座候。然れば末世の今に、天地・山川・五形・五倫・鳥獣・草木の名に至る迄皆通達せり。然雖も言葉の余り相違は遠国の上久しく通融絶えたる故也」）。なお、最近の[[遺伝子]]の研究で沖縄県民と九州以北の本土住民とは、同じ祖先を持つことが明らかになっている。[[高宮広土]]（[[鹿児島大学]]）が、沖縄の島々に人間が適応できたのは縄文中期後半から後期以降である為、10世紀から12世紀頃に農耕をする人々が九州から沖縄に移住したと指摘するように、近年の[[考古学]]などの研究も含めて[[南西諸島]]の住民の先祖は、九州南部から比較的新しい時期（10世紀前後）に南下して定住したものが主体であると推測されている{{Cite news|author=|url=http://www.asahi.com/culture/news_culture/TKY201004160274.html|title=ルーツ解明 沖縄に注目|newspaper=[[朝日新聞]]|publisher=|date=2010-04-16|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20100420201708/http://www.asahi.com/culture/news_culture/TKY201004160274.html|archivedate=2010-04-20}}{{Cite news|author=|url=http://www.asahi.com/culture/news_culture/TKY201004160274_01.html|title=ルーツ解明 沖縄に注目|newspaper=[[朝日新聞]]|publisher=|date=2010-04-16|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20110629171640/http://www.asahi.com/culture/news_culture/TKY201004160274_01.html|archivedate=2011-06-29}}。[[2021年]][[11月10日]]、[[マックス・プランク人類史科学研究所]]を中心とした、[[中華人民共和国|中国]]、[[日本]]、[[大韓民国|韓国]]、[[ヨーロッパ]]、[[ニュージーランド]]、[[ロシア]]、[[アメリカ合衆国|アメリカ]]の研究者を含む国際チームが『[[ネイチャー]]』に発表した論文によると、[[宮古島市]]の[[長墓遺跡]]の[[先史時代]]の[[人間の骨の一覧|人骨]]を[[DNA型鑑定|DNA分析]]したところ「100%[[縄文人]]」だったことが分かり、先史時代の[[先島諸島]]の人々は[[沖縄諸島]]から来たことを示す研究成果となった。また、[[言語学]]および[[考古学]]からは、[[中世]]（[[グスク時代]]、[[11世紀]]~[[15世紀]]）に[[九州]]から「本土日本人」が[[南西諸島|琉球列島]]に移住したことが推定でき、[[高宮広土]]（[[鹿児島大学]]）は、「結果として、[[琉球語|琉球方言]]の元となる言語を有した農耕民が本土から[[植民地|植民]]した。著名な『日本人二重構造論』を否定するという点で大変貴重だ」と指摘している{{Cite news|author=|url=https://www.okinawatimes.co.jp/articles/-/862140|title=宮古島先史の人々「北側の沖縄諸島から」「南から」説を覆す 人骨DNA分析で100%縄文人|newspaper=[[沖縄タイムス]]|publisher=|date=2021-11-12|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20211113042120/https://www.okinawatimes.co.jp/articles/-/862140|archivedate=2021-11-13}}{{Cite news|author=|url=https://www.kyushu-u.ac.jp/ja/researches/view/696/|title=トランスユーラシア言語は農耕と共に新石器時代に拡散した|newspaper=[[九州大学]]|publisher=|date=2021-11-26|archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20211127021902/https://www.kyushu-u.ac.jp/ja/researches/view/696/|archivedate=2021-11-27}}。}}。[[正徳 (日本)|正徳]]3年（[[1713年]]）の『[[琉球国由来記]]』には、「大昔、[[那覇港]]の海中から光が絶えず射していたので、調べてみると霊木であった。これを熊野権現の霊木とし、社寺を建てこの木を祀った」とある加治順人『沖縄の神社』p22　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日。 最初は那覇埠頭の地（現・那覇市西3丁目）にあったが、明治41年（[[1908年]]）、那覇港埠頭築港に伴い那覇市安里の[[安里八幡宮]]の隣地に遷座し、寺は那覇市住吉町に移転した。その時の社殿は琉球における[[神社建築]]の典型例として、[[昭和]]13年（[[1938年]]）[[国宝]]に指定されたが、[[沖縄戦]]で焼失した。戦後、比嘉真忠に「沖宮を復興せよ」と神託があり、沖宮霊木の根元は奥武山の天燈山と神示があった。昭和36年（[[1961年]]）に通堂町へ仮遷座し、同50年、現位置である[[奥武山公園]]内へ遷座された。 令和二年に[[神社本庁]]との被包括関係を解き、[[単立]]神社となった。 そのほか、先代宮司である比嘉真忠氏によって沖縄各地に伊計神社（[[うるま市]]与那城伊計218）、宜名真神社（[[国頭村]]字宜名真218）、[[恵比須神社 (那覇市)|恵比須神社]]（[[那覇市]]字安謝251）、神着宮（[[名護市]]字安部）が創建された。また、平成29年には[[伊平屋島]]に伊平屋天巌戸神社（[[伊平屋村]]字田名 念頭平松付近）が創建された。クマヤ洞窟の遥拝所の役割を果たす神社である。（広報いへや 2018年1月号）いずれも御祭神は[[天照大御神]]である。 == 交通 == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === モノレール === * [[沖縄都市モノレール]]（ゆいレール） [[奥武山公園駅]] === 路線バス === 各路線の詳細は、[[琉球バス交通]]、[[那覇バス]]の項目を参照 ;奥武山公園駅前バス停 :*11番（安岡宇栄原線） 那覇バス :*17番（石嶺（開南）線）　那覇バス :*101番（平和台安謝線） 那覇バス :*105番（豊見城市内一周線） 琉球バス交通 == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} {{琉球八社}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おきのくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:那覇市の建築物]] revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 00:28 (UTC)|ソートキー=沖縄おきのくう}} {{独自研究|date=2014年2月5日 (水) 00:28 (UTC)}} {{神社 |名称 = 沖宮 |画像 = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] |所在地 = [[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]奥武山町44 | 緯度度 = 26 | 緯度分 = 12 | 緯度秒 = 10.0 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 127 |経度分 = 40 | 経度秒 = 37.5 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP | 地図 = Japan Naha city |祭神 = 天受久女龍宮王御神
天龍大御神
天久臣乙女王御神
熊野三神 |社格 = 旧[[無格社]] |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = 琉球八社 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''沖宮'''（おきのぐう）は[[沖縄県]][[那覇市]]にある[[神社]]。[[琉球八社]]の一つであるが、[[近代社格制度]]では社格を与えられておらず、神社本庁に属さない[[単立]]の神社である。（[[無格社]]）。[[奥武山公園]]の東部に位置する。 == 祭神 == 天受久女龍宮王御神（てんじゅくめりゅうぐうおうおんかみ。[[天照大御神]]）・天龍大御神（てんりゅうおおおんかみ）・天久臣乙女王御神（あめくしんおとめおうおんかみ）・熊野三神（[[イザナミ|伊弉册尊]]・[[熊野権現|速玉男尊]]・[[事解男尊]]）を祀る。 [[明治]]の[[神仏分離]]までは「沖山三所権現」（沖の寺）と称し、[[神仏習合]]で[[阿弥陀如来]]・[[薬師如来]]・[[十一面観音]]を祭っていた。 *神代一代 **相殿中央の御座 *** 御先 女天神代　天受久女龍宮大御神（てんじゅくめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） *** 中　女天神代　天智門女龍宮大御神（あまちじょうめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） *** 今　女天神代　天受賀女龍宮大御神（てんじゅかめりゅうぐうおおおんかみ） **相殿左の御座 ** 父御神　天龍大御神（てんりゅうおおおんかみ） ** 母御神　天久臣乙女大御神（あめくしんおとめおおおんかみ） **相殿右の御座 *** 天芳子乙女大御神（てんぽうしおとめおおおんかみ） *** 天仁子乙女大御神（てんじんしおとめおおおんかみ） *** 天来子乙女大御神（てんらいしおとめおおおんかみ） **熊野三神 *** 伊弉冊尊（いざなみのみこと） *** 速玉男尊（はやたまをのみこと） *** 事解男尊（ことさかをのみこと） == 摂末社 == ; 末社 住吉神社 : 神代二代 : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 天風龍大神（あまふうりゅうおおかみ） - 子（ね）の神 表筒王 :* 天火龍大神（あまひりゅうおおかみ） - 丑（うし）の神 中筒王 : : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 表臣幸乙女大御神（うはしんこうおとめおおおんかみ） :* 中臣幸乙女大御神 : : 下二柱が御夫婦神 :* 天水龍大神（あますいりゅうおおかみ） - 寅（とら）の神 底筒王 :* 底臣幸乙女王御神（そこしんこうおとめおおおんかみ） ; 末社 辨財天宮（べんざいてんぐう） : 神世三代 :* 木龍宇具志久乙姫王（もくりゅうぐしおとひめおおお） - 辰（たつ）の神 :*: またの名を 辨天負百津姫神（べんてんよもつひめかみ）：ノロ神の御名 == 歴史 == (日本)|正徳]]3年（[[1713年]]）の『[[琉球国由来記]]』には、「大昔、[[那覇港]]の海中から光が絶えず射していたので、調べてみると霊木であった。これを熊野権現の霊木とし、社寺を建てこの木を祀った」とある加治順人『沖縄の神社』p22　ひるぎ社、2000年10月30日。 最初は那覇埠頭の地（現・那覇市西3丁目）にあったが、明治41年（[[1908年]]）、那覇港埠頭築港に伴い那覇市安里の[[安里八幡宮]]の隣地に遷座し、寺は那覇市住吉町に移転した。その時の社殿は琉球における[[神社建築]]の典型例として、[[昭和]]13年（[[1938年]]）[[国宝]]に指定されたが、[[沖縄戦]]で焼失した。戦後、比嘉真忠に「沖宮を復興せよ」と神託があり、沖宮霊木の根元は奥武山の天燈山と神示があった。昭和36年（[[1961年]]）に通堂町へ仮遷座し、同50年、現位置である[[奥武山公園]]内へ遷座された。 令和二年に[[神社本庁]]との被包括関係を解き、[[単立]]神社となった。 そのほか、先代宮司である比嘉真忠氏によって沖縄各地に伊計神社（[[うるま市]]与那城伊計218）、宜名真神社（[[国頭村]]字宜名真218）、[[恵比須神社 (那覇市)|恵比須神社]]（[[那覇市]]字安謝251）、神着宮（[[名護市]]字安部）が創建された。また、平成29年には[[伊平屋島]]に伊平屋天巌戸神社（[[伊平屋村]]字田名 念頭平松付近）が創建された。クマヤ洞窟の遥拝所の役割を果たす神社である。（広報いへや 2018年1月号）いずれも御祭神は[[天照大御神]]である。 == 交通 == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === モノレール === * [[沖縄都市モノレール]]（ゆいレール） [[奥武山公園駅]] === 路線バス === 各路線の詳細は、[[琉球バス交通]]、[[那覇バス]]の項目を参照 ;奥武山公園駅前バス停 :*11番（安岡宇栄原線） 那覇バス :*17番（石嶺（開南）線）　那覇バス :*101番（平和台安謝線） 那覇バス :*105番（豊見城市内一周線） 琉球バス交通 == 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{notelist}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} {{琉球八社}} {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おきのくう}} [[Category:沖縄県の神社]] [[Category:那覇市の建築物]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Oki-no-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{citation needed|Oki-no-gū}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}}

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Oki-no-gū}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Ryukyu Eight Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages without meta categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]]

Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image = | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[Izanami-no-Mikoto]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[en:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]]
