Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]]

Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]]

Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]]

Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]]

Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | prefecture = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[sn:Oki-no-gū]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]]

Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|qid=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qid=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}}

[[sn:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11552497]] Wikidata name: Oki-no-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image =File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[sn:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11552497]] Wikidata name: Oki-no-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image =File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} [[Category:completion tier 1]] {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] [[commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] [[sn:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11552497]] Wikidata name: Oki-no-gū [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image =File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located in [[:en:Naha|Naha]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]], Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: [[:en:Izanami|Izanami]], [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the [[:en:Battle of Okinawa|Battle of Okinawa]]. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, [[:en:Uruma|Uruma]]), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, [[:en:Naha|Naha]]), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on [[:en:Iheya Island|Iheya Island]]. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * [[:en:Okinawa Urban Monorail|Okinawa Urban Monorail]] (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see [[:en:Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu]] and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|WD=Q694313|lt=Uruma}}), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] [[co:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] [[sn:commons:Category:Oki-no-gū]][[ja:沖宮]][[zh:沖宮]] {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image =File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami}}, [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa}}. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|WD=Q694313|lt=Uruma}}), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha}}), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966|1=Naha}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|WD=Q694313|lt=Uruma|1=Uruma}}), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island|1=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail|1=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|1=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|WD=Q694313|lt=Uruma|1=Uruma}}), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island|1=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail|1=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|1=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] [[zh:%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE]] [[ja:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} [[zh:沖宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11552497}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]]

{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Oki Shrine | image =File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg | location = 44 Ounoyama-cho, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan | latitude degrees = 26 | type minutes = 12 | type seconds = 10.0 | N/S = N | longitude degrees = 127 | longitude minutes = 40 | longitude seconds = 37.5 | E/W = E | country code = JP | map = Japan Naha city | deity = Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami
Tenryu-Omikami
Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami
Kumano Sanshin | shrine Rank = Former [[unranked shrine]] | founded = | main hall = | other names = | notable for = [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]] | festival = | rituals = | location = {{ill|Naha|WD=Q181966|1=Naha}} | coordinates = {{Coord|26.202778|N|127.677083|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | part_of = {{ill|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|WD=Q11573652|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines}} }} '''Oki Shrine''' (沖宮, Oki-no-gū) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}, Japan. It is one of the [[Eight Ryukyu Shrines]], but was not Ranked in the [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] and is an independent shrine not affiliated with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. It is situated in the eastern part of [[Ounoyama Park]]. == Deities == Oki Shrine enshrines Tenjuku-Meryuuguo-no-Kami (天受久女龍宮王御神), Tenryu-Omikami (天龍大御神), Ame-Kushin-Otome-no-Kami (天久臣乙女王御神), and the Kumano Sanshin (熊野三神: {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|WD=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}}, [[Kumano Gongen|Hayatama-no-Mikoto]], and [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto]]). Before the [[Meiji]] [[Shinbutsu bunri]], the shrine was known as "Okiyama Sansho Gongen" and was a syncretic site that also worshiped [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]], [[:en:Yakushi Nyorai|Yakushi Nyorai]], and [[Kannon]]. == Sub-shrines == ; Suehiro Shrine : The second generation of gods: :* Tenfuuryu-Omikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) - God of the Rat, Omote Tsutsuo :* Tenhiryuu-Omikami (天火龍大神) - God of the Ox, Nakatsu Tsutsuo : The third generation of gods: :* Omote-Kouotome-Oomikami (表臣幸乙女大御神) :* Nakashinko-Ootome-Oomikami (中臣幸乙女大御神) : The fourth generation of gods: :* Tenmiryuu-Omikami (天水龍大神) - God of the Tiger, Sokotsu Tsutsuo :* Sokushinko-Otome-Oomikami (底臣幸乙女王御神) ; Benzaiten Shrine : The third generation of gods: :* Mokuryu-Gushiku-Otohime-Ookami (木龍宇具志久乙姫王) - God of the Dragon :*: Also known as Benzaiten-Yomotsuhime-Kami (弁天負百津姫神): the name of the Norokami. == History == The origin of the shrine is unknown, but historical records suggest it dates back to the time of [[Minamoto no Tametomo]]. According to the ''[[Records of the Ryukyu Kingdom]]'' (正徳3rd year , 1713), a sacred tree from which light continuously emanated was discovered in the sea near [[Naha Port]]. This tree was deemed sacred to Kumano Gongen, and a shrine was built to enshrine itJunhito Kaji, ''Shrines of Okinawa'', p. 22, Hirugi-sha, October 30, 2000. Initially, the shrine was located at the Naha Wharf (now Naha Nishimachi). In 1908, due to the construction of Naha Port, it was relocated next to [[Asato-Hachimangū‎]] in Naha. The temple was moved to Sumiyoshi-cho. The shrine building was designated a national treasure in 1938 as an example of Ryukyu shrine architecture but was destroyed in the {{ill|Battle of Okinawa|en|Battle of Okinawa|WD=Q192660|lt=Battle of Okinawa|1=Battle of Okinawa}}. After the war, Higa Mashō received a divine revelation to restore Oki Shrine at Ounoyama. The shrine was temporarily moved to Tondo-cho in 1961 and then relocated to its current location within Ounoyama Park in 1975. In 2020, the shrine ended its affiliation with the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and became an independent shrine. Former chief priest Higa Mashō also established several shrines throughout Okinawa, including Ike Shrine (218 Yonashiro, Ike, {{ill|Uruma|en|Uruma|WD=Q694313|lt=Uruma|1=Uruma}}), Ginama Shrine (218 Ginama, [[:en:Kunigami|Kunigami]]), [[Ebisu Shrine]] (251 Aja, {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|WD=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}}), and Kamitsuke Shrine (Abe, [[Nago]]). In 2017, Iheya Tenangado Shrine (near Tana, Iheya) was established on {{ill|Iheya Island|en|Iheya Island|WD=Q4518946|lt=Iheya Island|1=Iheya Island}}. == Access == {{Commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Osm box|w|554172923}} === Monorail === * {{ill|Okinawa Urban Monorail|en|Okinawa Urban Monorail|WD=Q993679|lt=Okinawa Urban Monorail|1=Okinawa Urban Monorail}} (Yui Rail) [[Ounoyama Koen Station]] === Bus === For details on routes, see {{ill|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|en|Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|WD=Q11573640|lt=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu|1=Ryukyu Bus Kotsu}} and [[Naha Bus]] ;Ounoyama Koen Station Bus Stop :* Route 11 (Yasukawa Ueohara Line) Naha Bus :* Route 17 (Ishimine Kainan Line) Naha Bus :* Route 101 (Heiwadai Aja Line) Naha Bus :* Route 105 (Tomigusuku City Loop Line) Ryukyu Bus Kotsu == Notes == === Annotations === {{notelist}} === References === {{Reflist}} {{Shinto side}} {{shinto-stub}} {{commonscat|Oki-no-gū}} {{Okinawan Shrines}} {{translated page|ja|沖宮|version=95809386|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[image:Oki-no-gū entrance.jpeg|250px]] {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} {{Commons category|Oki-no-gū}} {{wikidata link|Q11552497|co|commons:Category:Oki-no-gū|ja|沖宮|zh|沖宮|zh|%E6%B2%96%E5%AE%AE}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naha]] [[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:History of Naha]] [[Category:Kumano faith]] [[Category:Kumano shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okinawa Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with 50+ untranslated japanese characters]] [[Category:Pages with interwikis with duplicate languages]]
